title: Half-uncial script
subtitle: The early medieval hand that gives the modern lowercase its ascenders and descenders
excerpt: Half-uncial is a late Roman script, developed roughly from the third to seventh centuries, that first gave letters clear ascenders and descenders. The Irish and Northumbrian adaptations of half-uncial produced the distinctive insular scripts used in manuscripts such as the Book of Kells.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on uncial and half-uncial scripts. Johnston's analysis of the historical development from majuscule to half-uncial is the primary reference for this teaching. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: A late Roman and early medieval script that sits between the majuscule uncial and the fully developed minuscule. The first script in the Western tradition to give many letters recognisable ascenders and descenders.

The distinctive Irish and Northumbrian adaptations of half-uncial, characterised by strong compression, wedge-shaped serifs, and decorative embellishment. Used in manuscripts including the Book of Kells and the Lindisfarne Gospels.

content.text: sits between the Roman formal book hand (uncial) and the Carolingian minuscule that followed it. It was used across Europe from roughly the third to the eighth century. The most celebrated versions came from the Irish monastic scriptoria of the sixth and seventh centuries. The letterforms are early versions of the modern lowercase alphabet: the half-uncial b, d, g, and s are close to their modern forms.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: Half-uncial is written at 4 nib-widths x-height, the same as Foundational. The 3 is the standard practice size.

content.text: Half-uncial x-height: 4 nib-widths. Ascenders: 2 nib-widths above the x-height line. Descenders: 2 nib-widths below the baseline. The script is more compact in the vertical dimension than Carolingian, which gives it a denser, more closely woven appearance on the page.

Nib angle: 0 to 10 degrees from the horizontal (nearly flat). This very shallow angle is the most distinctive feature of the half-uncial hand. It produces broad, flat horizontals and narrow verticals, the opposite proportion from Foundational (which at 30 degrees produces fat, rounded strokes). Hold the pen almost horizontal to the baseline; this takes some adjustment if you are accustomed to Foundational angle.

The half-uncial a is an open, two-story form. Stroke 1: a nearly circular bowl, written anti-clockwise from the 2 o'clock position. The bowl is flat at the top and bottom due to the shallow nib angle. Stroke 2: a small angled tick at the top right, the entry to the following letter. No separate stem; the bowl rests directly on the baseline.

The half-uncial d has a small ascending stroke rather than a full ascender. Stroke 1: the bowl, as for o. Stroke 2: a short stem rising 1.5 nib-widths above the top of the bowl, finishing with a small wedge-shaped serif. The d, like the a, is a recognisably modern form.
The Irish insular adaptation compresses the letter width, adds strong wedge-shaped serifs on ascenders, and replaces the open bowl of the a with a closed geometric form. The

s are more angular than continental half-uncial. The nib angle is slightly higher (10 to 15 degrees) in the insular tradition. Facsimile pages of the Lindisfarne Gospels, now widely reproduced in teaching materials, give a good model.
